---
title: "Stanley-Burgner 2021"
featuredImage: "https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09869.QQP7OtwW-scaled.jpg"
startDate: "2021-08-22T00:00:00.000Z"
endDate: "2021-08-23T00:00:00.000Z"
tripPlan: content/trip-plans/stanley-burgner-2021.mdx
destinations:
  - route: content/routes/stanley-burgner.mdx
---

This was a LONG day. 24 hours and 20 minutes. This one's probably worth camping for since it's an exhausting, tough route!

## Overall notes

- Communications were actually pretty good, didn't bring radios and didn't really need them
- LOTS of crack climbing! Usually corner/weird cracks, but still, tons of jamming.

## Gear brought

- Doubles #0.3-2 and single #3-4, was perfect!
- 12 quickdraws with 8 alpine and 1 double alpine, was perfect!
- 60m rope

## Timeline

- 3:25 AM started
- 5:25 lake
- 7:45 top of pass
- 9:00 starting up final pass + 15 min bathroom break
- 9:30 saddle
- 9:35 ditching gear
- 9:55 barefoot
- 10:15 base
- 5:50 PM top
- 6:50 done rappelling
- 7:07 back at bags
- 3:45 AM (next day) back at car

## Hike in

We started hiking at 3:25 AM and reached the saddle a bit ahead of schedule.

![A tent in the water](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09848.HI7PYHCd-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09864.gPMXjk4P-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09865.qqoKWd6-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09869.QQP7OtwW-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09875.Zms9DSH5-1024x612.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09871.Nl6Q9sK-1024x683.jpg)

## Approach

We ditched gear near the saddle, including our hiking shoes, and consolidated to one shared small climbing pack with water+snacks+jackets. The approach from the saddle to the base was short, 20 mins at a slow barefoot pace on easy class 2/3 terrain.

![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09878.r65hNdG3-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09879.AFP6AVED-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09880.zDEdrEm-683x1024.jpg)

## Climb

t;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:123,&quot;y&quot;:160,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;10b&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:5,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:175,&quot;y&quot;:136,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;&lt;-   topoOverlaySrc="data:image/png;base64,"
/>

## P1-2 - 190'

Linking these was nice! P2 is easy and it felt pretty natural to just continue up. P1 was a little tricky, lots of jamming, a bit wide, but not bad, and pretty fun!

![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/IMG_7920.f6OmrV6f-768x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/IMG_7923.76jnjZJ-768x1024.jpg)

## P3 - 150+'

This is where the climb earns the PG-13 rating. Climb 5 feet up, and then traverse right and up on slab knobs, with limited to no protection opportunities. The knobs make it better than most alpine runout slab pitches though!

<RouteOverlay 
  imageSrc="https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09884.MgKMHPb1-1024x683.jpg" 
  topoData="{&quot;items&quot;:[{&quot;it&quot;:0,&quot;is&quot;:1.0757142857142856,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:3,&quot;cp&quot;:[{&quot;x&quot;:460,&quot;y&quot;:660},{&quot;x&quot;:430,&quot;y&quot;:495},{&quot;x&quot;:403,&quot;y&quot;:386},{&quot;x&quot;:537,&quot;y&quot;:347},{&quot;x&quot;:729,&quot;y&quot;:287},{&quot;x&quot;:768,&quot;y&quot;:234}],&quot;nl&quot;:10,&quot;fl&quot;:10,&quot;lc&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:5,&quot;is&quot;:1.0757142857142856,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:510,&quot;y&quot;:419,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;Traverse right\non slab knobs&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false}]}"
  topoOverlaySrc="data:image/png;base64,"
/>

In order to climb the 10b crack, I exited P3 on the left to find the base of the crack.

<RouteOverlay 
  imageSrc="https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09886.MFj2T3M-683x1024.jpg" 
  topoData="{&quot;items&quot;:[{&quot;it&quot;:3,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:2,&quot;x&quot;:35,&quot;y&quot;:360,&quot;w&quot;:18,&quot;h&quot;:18,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;&quot;},{&quot;it&quot;:5,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:12,&quot;y&quot;:307,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;10b\ncrack&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:0,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:3,&quot;cp&quot;:[{&quot;x&quot;:121,&quot;y&quot;:481},{&quot;x&quot;:101,&quot;y&quot;:403},{&quot;x&quot;:38,&quot;y&quot;:394},{&quot;x&quot;:43,&quot;y&quot;:380}],&quot;nl&quot;:10,&quot;fl&quot;:10,&quot;lc&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:0,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:3,&quot;cp&quot;:[{&quot;x&quot;:261,&quot;y&quot;:435},{&quot;x&quot;:322,&quot;y&quot;:317},{&quot;x&quot;:343,&quot;y&quot;:293}],&quot;nl&quot;:10,&quot;fl&quot;:10,&quot;lc&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:5,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:240,&quot;y&quot;:185,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;Normal P3/4 goes up gully&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false}]}"
  topoOverlaySrc="data:image/png;base64,"
/>

## P4 10b alt hand crack

Unfortunately, this hand crack is a bit big - mostly #3 size with some #2. I was still able to climb it with a single #3 though as there's spots where you can place #2's instead. I climbed it cleanly, but my foot was barely big enough to jam without slipping. Including the starting anchor, I used every single one of my cams (except maybe the 0.3's). It's a kinda long pitch!

At the top, there's no great anchor options, so I made the big reach step around to the right to connect back to the normal route and built an anchor below the chockstone, and extended my anchor so I could minimize the rope drag around the corner a bit. Still a super annoying diversion.

![Second half](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09888.sJnpUGzk-683x1024.jpg)
![Top](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09889.LocxKpO-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09891.WZNaVhPa-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09894.QxmbSWg-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09901.M3tmvgKU-683x1024.jpg)

## P4.2 - Chockstone

We did a tiny little pitch to get through the chockstone (like 10-15 feet). If we didn't do the 10b hand crack, we could have avoided doing this as a separate pitch.

The chockstone was actually quite physically strenuous! Lots of stemming and mantling to get up and through.

![From below...](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09897.VNaDHGOE-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09902.ch5h3Ey-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09904.BxMICu4z-1024x683.jpg)

## P5

The stemming "chimney" corner pitch. It protected decently well and felt pretty safe, but was super exhausting. Definitely can't have a backpack! Lots of slowly inching upward. I dropped a rope strand to haul the backpack up.

<RouteOverlay 
  imageSrc="https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09905.ae6ZuD21-683x1024.jpg" 
  topoData="{&quot;items&quot;:[{&quot;it&quot;:0,&quot;is&quot;:1.0514285714285714,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:3,&quot;cp&quot;:[{&quot;x&quot;:420,&quot;y&quot;:971},{&quot;x&quot;:421,&quot;y&quot;:655},{&quot;x&quot;:392,&quot;y&quot;:469},{&quot;x&quot;:369,&quot;y&quot;:394},{&quot;x&quot;:389,&quot;y&quot;:343},{&quot;x&quot;:383,&quot;y&quot;:310},{&quot;x&quot;:348,&quot;y&quot;:266},{&quot;x&quot;:334,&quot;y&quot;:241}],&quot;nl&quot;:10,&quot;fl&quot;:10,&quot;lc&quot;:false}]}"
  topoOverlaySrc="data:image/png;base64,"
/>

![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09907.m4enxQX5-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09908.RT4J3z2X-1024x683.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09909.A8kao4YA-825x1024.jpg)

## P5 to P6

We moved the belay quickly to have a closer/better belay for P6.

![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09911.bXT9h5z-200x300.jpg)

## P6.1

This pitch is LONG. Midway up, there was a really wide crack (body size) that maybe had some corner smearing options, but looked unprotectable. I chose to climb to the right instead and climbed up a dirty crack to a nice belay ledge. I had already used a lot of gear, so I stopped to belay there.

## P6.2

From my belay ledge, I had to do a really wide reach step around the corner to return to the route, but I actually managed to make the move. The rest of the route has a lot more corner jams and is super strenuous!

![Alexis about to start](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09912.B1B6BNkx-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09913.PdX4DayY-683x1024.jpg)
![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2021/08/DSC09914.lq8i2WEz-1024x683.jpg)
